channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Sparkling, Stunning New Romance Books
item.description: Our columnist on three sparkling new romances.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Waite

item.title: Book Review: ‘Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America,’ by Michael Kimmel
item.description: In “Playmakers,” Michael Kimmel traces, and celebrates, the immigrant roots of the American toy industry. (Batteries not included.)
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America (Book)

item.title: The Month’s Best New Mystery Books
item.description: Our critic on three terrific new mysteries and a gem-filled story collection.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Weinman
item.category: The Tree of Light and Flowers (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Everybody’s Fly,’ by Fab 5 Freddy
item.description: As his new memoir demonstrates, he himself would achieve fame as a visual artist, filmmaker, TV host and formative tastemaker.
item.dc:creator: Alan Light
item.category: Everybody's Fly: A Life of Art, Music, and Changing the Culture (Book)

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Who Is Antigone? The 2500-Year-Old Rebel With a Cause.
item.description: “Antigone” gave us the original “bad girl,” but its themes go beyond that. How do adaptations keep making Sophocles’ ideas about democracy and theater new?
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Feather Wars,’ by James H. McCommons
item.description: In “The Feather Wars,” James H. McCommons pays tribute to the nation’s first conservationists.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Hammer
item.category: The Feather Wars: And the Great Crusade to Save America's Birds (Book)
item.media:description: The Delineator, a creation of the Butterick Publishing Company, set fashion trends and promoted its sewing patterns to home seamstresses. Here, a page highlights the Victorian craze for elaborate plumage, one of several fads to threaten bird species at the time.

item.title: Don DeLillo’s Hockey Novel, ‘Amazons,’ Will Return to Stores
item.description: You’re welcome.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: ‘Monte Cristo’ Review: A Tale of Betrayal, on Two Counts
item.description: An adaptation has a twist that doesn’t track, and songs that benefit from an excellent cast, including Norm Lewis, Sierra Boggess and Adam Jacobs.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: What the ‘Project Hail Mary’ Directors Learned From a Firing
item.description: Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, best known for animations like the “Spider-Verse” films, took lessons from “Solo: A Star Wars Story,” a project from which they were dismissed.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times
item.media:description: Phil Lord, left, and Christopher Miller. “It was almost contrarian to make a movie that’s so affirming about a topic that is on everyone’s mind, which is this idea that we’re doomed,” Lord said.

item.title: 10 Kids’ Books for Fans of The Baby-Sitters Club
item.description: Ten recommendations for fans of Ann M. Martin’s iconic paperback series.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Hubert Swan
item.category: A Babysitters Guide to Monster Hunting (Book)

item.title: A.I. Is Writing Fiction. Publishers Are Unprepared.
item.description: Book publishing has few safeguards in place to prevent the unwitting publication of a novel heavily generated by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Horror Novel ‘Shy Girl’ Canceled Over Suspected A.I. Use
item.description: Its publisher, Hachette, will not release the novel in the United States and will discontinue its U.K. edition, citing its commitment to “original creative expression and storytelling.”
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: The cover of the U.K. edition of “Shy Girl,” by Mia Ballard, which was published by Hachette last year.

item.title: Review: ‘The Wild Party’ Has a Ball at City Center
item.description: Encores! revisits a Jazz Age tale of debauchery, with showstoppers from Jasmine Amy Rogers, Adrienne Warren, Jordan Donica, Tonya Pinkins and others.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Encores! Great American Musicals in Concert (Series)
item.media:description: Adrienne Warren as Kate, left, and Jasmine Amy Rogers as Queenie in the Encores! staging of “The Wild Party” at New York City Center in Manhattan.

item.title: Books Our Editors Love This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.

item.title: 2 Chainz, One of Hip-Hop’s Cheekiest Lyricists, Gets Serious
item.description: The rapper known for his quirky turns of phrase and malapropisms is trying his hand at a memoir.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.category: The Voice in My Head Is God (Book)
item.media:credit: Alexander J. Rotondo for The New York Times

item.title: Our Spring Book Recommendations
item.description: A few editors from the New York Times’s Book Review give their recommendations for what new releases you should be reading this spring.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Harlan, MJ Franklin, Joumana Khatib, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Great Vampire Books
item.description: The best-selling author Kiersten White recommends novels about everyone’s favorite undead bloodsuckers, by Anne Rice, Silvia Moreno Garcia and more.
item.dc:creator: Kiersten White

item.title: T. Kingfisher on Her Favorite Books and Her Disgusting New Novel
item.description: “I have written six books and counting just because I was very annoyed at how a character was written in a video game,” she says. Her “disgusting” new novel is “Wolf Worm.”
item.category: A Wizard's Guide to Defensive Baking (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Paradiso 17,’ by Hannah Lillith Assadi
item.description: “Paradiso 17,” by Hannah Lillith Assadi, considers the toll of displacement through the tale of a Palestinian émigré.
item.dc:creator: Zain Khalid

item.title: Andy Weir on the Science That Inspired His Novel ‘Project Hail Mary’
item.description: Andy Weir discusses his science-fueled novel “Project Hail Mary,” which has been adapted into a film that opens in theaters on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Jonathan Olley/Amazon MGM Studios, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Andy Weir, author of the sci-fi book “Project Hail Mary,” on the set of the movie adaptation, which he produced and which opens Friday.

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Scandal in Königsberg,’ by Christopher Clark
item.description: A new book by the historian Christopher Clark chronicles a nearly 200-year-old scandal with echoes of the present day.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.media:description: Königsberg, once the home of Immanuel Kant, appeared past its prime in the 1830s when it was caught up in a sex scandal.

item.title: Book Review: ‘We’ and ‘The People Can Fly,’ by Joshua Bennett
item.description: Joshua Bennett’s two new collections, “We” and “The People Can Fly,” take different paths to the same destination.
item.dc:creator: Holly Bass
item.category: We (The People of the United States) (Book)
The People Can Fly: American Promise, Black Prodigies, and the Greatest Miracle of All Time (Book)

item.title: Alfredo Bryce Echenique, 87, Dies; Novelist Bared Peru’s Privileged Class
item.description: “The other Peruvian” (alongside Mario Vargas Llosa), he exposed the heedlessness of the upper crust, which he knew well, and the suffering of the underclasses.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Alfredo Bryce Echenique in 1999 in Paris, where he long lived in self-imposed exile. “His own social class loathed him,” his biographer said. “He would say, ‘The elitists are deeply ignorant.’”

item.title: Albert Zuckerman, Literary Agent and ‘Hero of the Blockbuster,’ Dies at 94
item.description: During his 50-year career, he represented dozens of best-selling authors, including Ken Follett, Stephen Hawking and Michael Lewis.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Albert Zuckerman in 2015. He started the Writers House literary agency in 1973, working out of a 100-square-foot office above a porn store in Times Square.

item.title: Len Deighton, Author of Espionage Best-Sellers, Dies at 97
item.description: His Cold War thrillers “The Ipcress File” and “Funeral in Berlin” brought a documentary-style realism to the spy genre.
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:description: Len Deighton in 1986. He wrote “The Ipcress File” in reaction to the James Bond novels of Ian Fleming, which he regarded as cartoonish and morally simplistic.

item.title: John Lithgow’s Career Spans 200 Roles — From ‘3rd Rock’ to Roald Dahl
item.description: Is there anyone John Lithgow can’t — or won’t — play?
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:credit: Raphael Gaultier for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a career stretching toward 200 roles, John Lithgow has played King Lear, a serial killer, a corrupt cardinal, a dance-banning reverend, an alien and Winston Churchill (twice).

item.title: Book Review: ‘Chain of Ideas,’ by Ibram X. Kendi
item.description: In “Chain of Ideas,” Ibram X. Kendi argues that a modern form of xenophobia has come to dominate conservative movements across the world.
item.dc:creator: Sam Adler-Bell
item.category: Chain of Ideas: The Origins of Our Authoritarian Age (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Salt Lakes,’ by Caroline Tracey
item.description: In a new book, Caroline Tracey explores the mysteries and beauty of salt lakes.
item.dc:creator: Robert Sullivan
item.media:credit: Alex Goodlett for The New York Times
item.media:description: After first encountering the eerie beauty of salt lakes, Caroline Tracey devoted herself to their history and future.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Sisters in Yellow,’ by Mieko Kawakami
item.description: Mieko Kawakami’s novel “Sisters in Yellow” follows a group of dreaming and scheming young women through society’s margins.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner

item.title: Book Review: ‘Stay Alive,’ by Ian Buruma
item.description: In “Stay Alive,” Ian Buruma paints a picture of the city dwellers who survived in Germany under the Nazis.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Peraino
item.media:description: The actress and singer Friedel Schuster performing in Berlin in 1940.
